cards.title: Code Red
cards.body: The term 'code red' originated in the 1970s at the NASA Johnson Space Center, where it signified a critical issue with the space shuttle's computer system.

cards.title: First Compiler
cards.body: The first compiler was written by Grace Hopper in 1952, revolutionizing programming by allowing developers to write code in higher-level languages.

cards.title: Open Source
cards.body: The term 'open source' was first coined in 1998 by Christine Peterson, a futurist and lecturer, to describe software that is freely available and modifiable.

cards.title: Bug Origin
cards.body: The term 'bug' originated from a moth that got stuck in the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947, causing it to malfunction.

cards.title: First Virus
cards.body: The first computer virus, 'Creeper', was discovered in 1971 and replicated itself on the ARPANET network, a precursor to the modern internet.

cards.title: AI Winter
cards.body: The 'AI winter' of the 1980s occurred when funding for artificial intelligence research decreased due to a lack of progress and high expectations.

cards.title: First Algorithm
cards.body: The first algorithm was developed by ancient Greek mathematician Euclid, who described a method for finding the greatest common divisor of two numbers.

cards.title: Code Review
cards.body: Code review, a crucial step in software development, was first introduced in the 1970s by IBM, to ensure that code met certain standards and was free of errors.

cards.title: First Programming Language
cards.body: Short Code, developed in the 1940s, is considered one of the first high-level programming languages, simplifying the process of writing code.

cards.title: Debugging
cards.body: The term 'debugging' was popularized by Admiral Grace Hopper, who found a moth stuck in a relay switch of the Harvard Mark II computer, coining the term.
